name: AP Microeconomics
description: Master the full AP Microeconomics curriculum — from scarcity and opportunity cost to market failure and government intervention. Covers all 6 CED units: Basic Economic Concepts, Supply and Demand, Production Cost and the Perfect Competition Model, Imperfect Competition, Factor Markets, and Market Failure and the Role of Government. Aligned to the College Board AP Microeconomics Course and Exam Description. Includes elasticity calculations, profit maximization rules (MR=MC), market structure comparisons, game theory basics, and welfare analysis.

facts.quizQuestion: What fundamental economic concept describes the condition where unlimited human wants exceed the limited resources available to fulfill them?
facts.explanation: Scarcity is the foundational concept of economics — resources (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship) are finite while human desires are unlimited, forcing every individual and society to make trade-offs in allocating what they have.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the collective term for the four categories of resources — land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship — used to produce goods and services?
facts.explanation: The four factors of production are the inputs that drive all economic activity. Land encompasses natural resources, labor is human effort, capital is manufactured tools and machinery, and entrepreneurship is the organizing force that combines the other three to create value.

facts.quizQuestion: What type of intangible investment refers to the skills, knowledge, experience, and education embodied in workers that increases their productivity?
facts.explanation: Human capital is the accumulated skills and knowledge workers develop through education, training, and experience. Unlike physical capital (machines, tools), human capital resides in people — investing in it raises productivity and lifetime earnings, and is a key driver of economic growth.

facts.quizQuestion: Because resources are scarce, every economic decision involves giving up one thing to obtain another. What is the general term for this unavoidable give-and-take in decision-making?
facts.explanation: A trade-off is the situation where obtaining more of one thing requires accepting less of something else. All economic decisions involve trade-offs — a government spending more on defense must spend less on education; a student studying more has less leisure time. Scarcity makes trade-offs universal.

facts.quizQuestion: What economic concept refers to the value of the next-best alternative that must be forgone when making any choice?
facts.explanation: Opportunity cost is the true cost of any choice — it measures what you give up by choosing one option over the best alternative. If you spend an evening studying, the opportunity cost is the movie you didn't watch or the job shift you didn't take. It includes both explicit (dollar) costs and implicit (time/foregone benefit) costs.

facts.quizQuestion: Economists assume rational decision-makers compare the extra benefit of doing one more unit of an activity against the extra cost of that unit. What is this decision-making framework called?
facts.explanation: Marginal analysis is the economic method of deciding whether to do a little more or a little less of something by comparing marginal benefit (MB) to marginal cost (MC). A rational person does more of an activity when MB > MC and stops when MB = MC. This logic drives everything from individual choices to firm output decisions.

facts.quizQuestion: According to marginal analysis, a rational economic agent should continue an activity up to the point where what condition holds between the incremental this and incremental cost?
facts.explanation: Rational decision-makers maximize net benefit by continuing any activity as long as marginal benefit exceeds marginal cost, and stopping exactly when MB = MC. Doing less leaves unrealized gains; doing more incurs costs greater than benefits. This rule applies to consumers, firms, and policymakers alike.

facts.quizQuestion: What economic graph shows all combinations of two goods that an economy can produce when using all its resources fully and efficiently?
facts.explanation: The production possibilities curve (PPC) maps the maximum output combinations achievable with given resources and technology. Points on the curve are efficient; points inside are inefficient (wasted resources); points outside are currently unattainable. It visually represents scarcity, trade-offs, and opportunity costs.

facts.quizQuestion: A point located inside (below) the production possibilities curve indicates that the economy is producing at what type of level relative to its potential?
facts.explanation: Any point inside the PPC represents inefficiency — the economy is not using all its resources fully (perhaps due to unemployment, idle capital, or poor resource allocation). Moving from an interior point to the PPC itself represents gaining output with no additional resources, simply by eliminating waste.

facts.quizQuestion: A point located outside (beyond) the production possibilities curve represents an output combination that is currently what?
facts.explanation: Points outside the PPC exceed what is achievable given current resources and technology. Such combinations are unattainable today, but the PPC can shift outward — making previously unattainable points possible — if technology improves or the quantity/quality of resources increases.

facts.quizQuestion: A straight-line (linear) production possibilities curve indicates that resources are equally productive in both uses, meaning what type of implicit cost?
facts.explanation: A linear PPC has a constant slope, meaning giving up one unit of Good A always yields the same gain in Good B regardless of where you are on the curve. This occurs when resources are perfectly substitutable between the two goods. In reality, most PPCs are bowed outward, reflecting increasing opportunity costs.

facts.quizQuestion: A production possibilities curve that bows outward (is concave to the origin) reflects what economic principle about the cost of reallocating resources?
facts.explanation: A bowed-out PPC reflects the law of increasing opportunity costs: as more resources are devoted to producing one good, the opportunity cost per additional unit rises. This happens because resources are not perfectly adaptable — the best resources for Good A are shifted first, then progressively less-suited resources, making each additional unit costlier.

facts.quizQuestion: When an economy experiences a major technological improvement that raises productivity across all sectors, in which direction does the production possibilities curve shift?
facts.explanation: Improvements in technology, increases in the quantity of resources, or improvements in resource quality all shift the PPC outward — expanding what the economy can produce. Outward shifts represent economic growth: combinations that were previously unattainable become achievable.

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes a producer's ability to produce a good using fewer resources (or in less time) than another producer?
facts.explanation: Absolute advantage means producing more output with the same inputs, or producing the same output with fewer inputs. Country A has an absolute advantage over Country B in wheat if it can grow more wheat per acre. However, absolute advantage alone does not determine who should specialize — that role belongs to comparative advantage.

facts.quizQuestion: What type of productive edge does a producer have when they can produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another producer?
facts.explanation: Comparative advantage is the key to understanding trade: even if one producer is absolutely better at everything, both parties gain by specializing in what they produce at the lowest opportunity cost and trading. This insight, often attributed to David Ricardo, explains why trade is mutually beneficial even between unequal trading partners.

facts.quizQuestion: Which 19th-century British economist formally developed the theory of comparative advantage, demonstrating that nations benefit from trade even when one is absolutely better at producing everything?
facts.explanation: David Ricardo (1772–1823) introduced comparative advantage in his 1817 work 'Principles of Political Economy and Taxation.' His famous example compared England and Portugal producing cloth and wine, showing that specialization and trade based on relative (not absolute) costs raises total output for both nations.

distractors: Equal to the lower opportunity cost
Equal to the higher opportunity cost
Determined by supply and demand alone
Equal to average of both opportunity costs
facts.quizQuestion: For trade to be mutually beneficial, the agreed-upon exchange rate between two goods must fall within what range?
facts.explanation: For both parties to gain from trade, the terms of trade must be set between each country's opportunity cost of production. If Country A's opportunity cost of wheat is 2 cloth and Country B's is 4 cloth, the terms must be between 2 and 4 cloth per wheat — otherwise one country would be better off producing the good domestically.

facts.quizQuestion: When countries specialize in producing goods in which they have a productive edge and then exchange them, what economic term describes the improvement in total output?
facts.explanation: Gains from trade are the increases in total consumption available when countries specialize and trade rather than try to produce everything themselves. By doing what each does at lowest opportunity cost and exchanging, the combined output exceeds what either could produce alone — a powerful argument for free trade.

facts.quizQuestion: What economic law states that, all else equal, when the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded falls, and when the price falls, the quantity demanded rises?
facts.explanation: The law of demand describes the inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded. When price rises, two effects reinforce each other: the income effect (buyers can afford less) and the substitution effect (the good is now relatively more expensive than alternatives). The result is an downward-sloping demand curve.

facts.quizQuestion: When a good's price rises and consumers feel poorer because their purchasing power has fallen, causing them to buy less even without switching to substitutes, which demand effect is at work?
facts.explanation: The income effect explains part of the law of demand: when a price rises, the consumer's real income (purchasing power) falls — they can afford fewer goods overall. For normal goods, this reduces quantity demanded. The income effect is especially significant for goods that represent a large portion of the consumer's budget.

acceptableAlternatives: Substitution effect of a price change
facts.quizQuestion: When coffee prices rise and consumers switch to drinking tea because tea is now relatively cheaper, which demand effect are they exhibiting?
facts.explanation: The substitution effect occurs when a good's price rises, making it relatively more expensive compared to substitute goods. Rational consumers switch toward the now cheaper alternatives, reducing quantity demanded of the pricier good. Along with the income effect, the substitution effect underlies the downward slope of the demand curve.

facts.quizQuestion: For a good where demand increases when consumer income rises and decreases when consumer income falls, what type of good is it classified as?
facts.explanation: A normal good has a positive income elasticity of demand — as income rises, people buy more of it. Most goods are normal goods: steak, vacations, new cars. When income falls, demand for normal goods decreases. This contrasts with inferior goods, where demand actually rises as income falls (e.g., ramen noodles, bus rides).

facts.quizQuestion: When consumer income increases and demand for a particular good decreases (because people can now afford better alternatives), what type of good is it?
facts.explanation: Inferior goods have a negative income elasticity of demand: as incomes rise, people switch away from them toward preferred substitutes. Classic examples include generic-brand foods, used clothing, and bus rides. Understanding the inferior/normal distinction is essential for predicting how demand curves shift when the economy changes.

facts.quizQuestion: Printers and ink cartridges, hot dogs and hot dog buns, and cars and gasoline are all examples of what type of goods, where a rise in the price of one reduces demand for the other?
facts.explanation: Complements are goods used together — demand for them moves in opposite directions when one's price changes. If the price of printers rises, people buy fewer printers and consequently fewer ink cartridges. A rise in the price of one complement shifts the demand curve for the other leftward. This is a key demand shifter tested on the AP exam.

facts.quizQuestion: Pepsi and Coca-Cola, butter and margarine, and Uber and taxis are all examples of what type of goods, where a rise in the price of one increases demand for the other?
facts.explanation: Substitutes satisfy similar needs — when the price of one rises, consumers switch to the other, increasing its demand. If Pepsi prices rise, demand for Coca-Cola shifts rightward. A rise in the price of a substitute shifts demand for the good in question to the right (increases demand). This is a key demand shifter on the AP exam.

acceptableAlternatives: Tastes, Related goods, Income, Buyers, Expectations
facts.quizQuestion: What mnemonic acronym is commonly used to remember the five non-price determinants that shift the demand curve (Tastes, Related goods prices, Income, Buyers in the market, Expectations)?
facts.explanation: TRIBE helps students recall all five demand shifters: Tastes/preferences, Related goods (substitute and complement prices), Income (normal vs. inferior goods), Buyers (number of consumers in the market), and Expectations (about future prices or income). A change in any TRIBE factor shifts the entire demand curve, while a price change causes a movement along the existing curve.

facts.quizQuestion: When the price of pizza rises from $10 to $12, causing consumers to buy fewer pizzas, is this represented graphically as a shift of the demand curve or as what type of change?
facts.explanation: A change in the good's own price causes a movement along the existing demand curve — quantity demanded changes but the curve itself does not move. This is called a 'change in quantity demanded.' Only changes in non-price determinants (TRIBE factors) shift the entire demand curve to a new position, called a 'change in demand.'

facts.quizQuestion: What economic law states that, all else equal, when the price of a good rises, producers are willing to supply a greater quantity, and when the price falls, they supply less?
facts.explanation: The law of supply describes the positive relationship between price and quantity supplied. Higher prices make production more profitable, incentivizing producers to increase output. This results in an upward-sloping supply curve. The law holds for most goods but can have exceptions in unusual markets (e.g., labor supply can bend backward at very high wages).

acceptableAlternatives: Resource prices, Other goods prices, Technology, Taxes/subsidies, Expectations, Number of sellers
facts.quizQuestion: What mnemonic acronym is used to remember the six non-price determinants that shift the supply curve (Resource prices, Other goods prices, Technology, Taxes/Subsidies, Expectations, Number of sellers)?
facts.explanation: ROTTEN captures all six supply shifters: Resource prices (input costs), Other goods prices (opportunity costs for producers), Technology (productivity), Taxes and subsidies (costs vs. incentives), Expectations (anticipated future prices), and Number of sellers. A change in any ROTTEN factor shifts the entire supply curve, while a change in the good's own price moves quantity supplied along the existing curve.

facts.quizQuestion: When a technological breakthrough makes it cheaper to produce a good, in which direction does the supply curve shift?
facts.explanation: A cost-reducing technological improvement allows producers to supply more at every price level — the supply curve shifts rightward (supply increases). Conversely, if production costs rise (e.g., higher input prices, new regulations), the supply curve shifts leftward. Rightward supply shifts push equilibrium price down and quantity up.

facts.quizQuestion: When the price of steel rises, raising the cost of manufacturing automobiles, in which direction does the supply curve for automobiles shift?
facts.explanation: Higher input (resource) prices raise production costs, reducing the profitability of supplying each quantity. Producers are willing to supply less at every price — the supply curve shifts leftward (supply decreases). A leftward supply shift raises the equilibrium price and lowers equilibrium quantity.

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the market condition where the quantity supplied equals the quantity demanded, resulting in a stable price with no tendency to change?
facts.explanation: Equilibrium is the price-quantity combination where the market 'clears' — there are no unsold goods piling up and no buyers going unsatisfied. At equilibrium, the plans of buyers and sellers are perfectly coordinated. Markets naturally tend toward equilibrium through price adjustments — the invisible hand mechanism described by Adam Smith.

facts.quizQuestion: When the market price is above the equilibrium price, causing the quantity supplied to exceed the quantity demanded, what market condition exists?
facts.explanation: A surplus (excess supply) occurs when price is too high — producers want to sell more than buyers want to buy at that price. Unsold inventory builds up, putting downward pressure on price. In free markets, the price falls until surplus is eliminated and the market returns to equilibrium. Price floors can create persistent surpluses.

facts.quizQuestion: When the market price is below the equilibrium price, causing the quantity demanded to exceed the quantity supplied, what market condition results?
facts.explanation: A shortage (excess demand) occurs when price is too low — buyers want to purchase more than producers are willing to supply at that price. Long lines, waiting lists, or bidding wars develop. In free markets, price rises until shortage is eliminated. Price ceilings set below equilibrium create persistent shortages.

facts.quizQuestion: If consumer incomes rise and the good is a normal good, causing demand to increase (shift right) with supply unchanged, what happens to equilibrium price and the output level?
facts.explanation: A rightward shift in demand (demand increases) with supply held constant creates a new equilibrium at a higher price AND higher quantity. The demand curve shift creates a shortage at the old price, which bids price up until the market clears at the new intersection. This is the standard demand-increase analysis for AP free-response questions.

facts.quizQuestion: If a technological improvement reduces production costs, causing supply to increase (shift right) with demand unchanged, what happens to equilibrium price and the output level?
facts.explanation: A rightward shift in supply (supply increases) with demand held constant creates a new equilibrium at a lower price but higher quantity. The supply shift creates a surplus at the old price, which drives price down until a new market-clearing quantity is reached. This is the standard supply-increase analysis for the AP exam.

facts.correctAnswer: Quantity rises, price change is indeterminate
distractors: Both price and quantity are indeterminate
facts.quizQuestion: When both the demand curve and the supply curve shift rightward simultaneously (both increase), what can definitely be concluded about the new equilibrium?
facts.explanation: When both demand and supply increase, equilibrium quantity definitely rises — both shifts push quantity higher. However, the effect on price is indeterminate: the demand increase pushes price up while the supply increase pushes price down. The net effect on price depends on the relative magnitude of each shift. This 'indeterminate' result is a classic AP exam double-shift question.

facts.correctAnswer: Opportunity cost of one good in terms of the other
distractors: The price ratio of the two goods
facts.quizQuestion: On a production possibilities curve, the absolute value of the slope at any point represents what economic measure?
facts.explanation: The slope of the PPC measures how much of Good Y must be given up to produce one more unit of Good X — exactly the opportunity cost. On a linear PPC, slope (and opportunity cost) is constant throughout. On a bowed-out PPC, the slope steepens as you move along the curve, reflecting increasing opportunity costs as resources become increasingly specialized.

facts.correctAnswer: The producer with the lower opportunity cost
acceptableAlternatives: Whoever has the lower opportunity cost
The one with lower opportunity cost
distractors: The producer with the higher output capacity
The producer with lower total cost
The country with the stronger currency
facts.quizQuestion: When determining which this should specialize in and export a particular good, economists recommend that production be assigned to whom?
facts.explanation: Comparative advantage is determined by opportunity cost, not absolute productivity. If Country A gives up 2 units of cloth to make 1 unit of wheat, and Country B gives up 3 units of cloth for 1 unit of wheat, Country A has comparative advantage in wheat (lower opportunity cost). Country A should specialize in and export wheat regardless of whether it's absolutely better at both goods.

facts.quizQuestion: Which 18th-century Scottish economist coined the metaphor of the 'invisible hand' to describe how self-interested market participants unintentionally promote the general welfare of society?
facts.explanation: Adam Smith introduced the 'invisible hand' concept in 'The Wealth of Nations' (1776), arguing that competitive markets coordinate the self-interested actions of buyers and sellers to produce socially beneficial outcomes — without any central planner directing resources. This foundational insight underpins free-market economics and is directly relevant to Unit 1 of AP Microeconomics.

facts.quizQuestion: Machines, tools, factories, computers, and infrastructure that are used to produce other goods and services are collectively classified as what type of resource?
facts.explanation: Physical capital (sometimes just 'capital') is the manufactured, human-made resource used in production — it is itself a produced good used to produce other goods. Unlike financial capital (money), physical capital directly contributes to productive capacity. Investment in physical capital shifts the PPC outward over time.

facts.quizQuestion: Which factor of production refers to the risk-taking, organizing ability of individuals who combine land, labor, and capital to create new businesses and bear the uncertainty of profit or loss?
facts.explanation: Entrepreneurship is the fourth factor of production — the organizing and risk-bearing function that brings the other three together in pursuit of profit. Entrepreneurs innovate, make key decisions, and absorb the financial risk of failure. Without entrepreneurship, resources would exist but not be combined into productive enterprises.

facts.quizQuestion: What Latin phrase used throughout economics means 'holding all other variables constant,' allowing economists to isolate the effect of one variable at a time?
facts.explanation: Ceteris paribus (Latin for 'other things being equal') is the analytical technique of isolating one variable while holding everything else constant. It allows economists to state laws like 'when price rises, quantity demanded falls — ceteris paribus.' Without this assumption, multiple simultaneous changes would make it impossible to identify cause-and-effect relationships.

facts.quizQuestion: What branch of economics focuses on the decision-making of individual consumers, firms, and markets — as opposed to economy-wide aggregates like GDP or unemployment?
facts.explanation: Microeconomics examines how individuals and firms make decisions about allocating scarce resources, how prices are determined in markets, and how market structure affects outcomes. It contrasts with macroeconomics, which studies economy-wide phenomena. AP Microeconomics tests micro concepts: supply/demand, production costs, market structures, and market failures.

facts.quizQuestion: The statement 'When the minimum wage increases, teenage employment falls' is a factual claim that can be tested against data. What type of economic analysis does it represent?
facts.explanation: Positive economics deals with factual, objective, testable claims about how the economy is or works — 'what is.' It doesn't say whether outcomes are good or bad. Normative economics involves value judgments about 'what ought to be.' The distinction matters: disagreements about positive statements can be resolved with data, while normative disagreements reflect different values.

facts.quizQuestion: The statement 'The government should increase the minimum wage to reduce poverty' reflects a value judgment about what policy ought to be done. What type of economic statement is this?
facts.explanation: Normative economics involves value-based judgments about what should happen — policy recommendations that depend on ethical priorities, not just facts. The word 'should' is a telltale indicator. Unlike positive statements (which are testable), normative disagreements cannot be settled by data alone — they reflect different values or goals about fairness, efficiency, and social priorities.

facts.quizQuestion: In a market economy, prices perform three functions: they allocate resources, provide incentives, and transmit information. What is the function called when a rising oil price tells producers to drill more and consumers to conserve?
facts.explanation: Prices act as signals that convey information about relative scarcity and value — without any central authority needing to collect or process that information. A rising price signals that a good is becoming scarcer or more demanded, directing resources toward production and discouraging consumption. This information-transmitting function of prices is a key argument for market economies over central planning.

facts.quizQuestion: If consumers expect the price of a good to rise sharply next month, they tend to buy more of it today. In which direction does current demand shift?
facts.explanation: Expectations of future price increases cause consumers to buy more today to avoid paying higher prices later — this shifts current demand rightward (increases demand), raising current equilibrium price and quantity. Expectations are one of the TRIBE demand shifters. This effect is especially visible in housing markets when buyers rush in anticipating price increases.

facts.quizQuestion: When a large population of new buyers enters a market (such as when a city's population grows rapidly), in which direction does market demand shift?
facts.explanation: The number of buyers in the market is one of the five TRIBE demand shifters. More buyers mean higher total market demand at every price — the demand curve shifts rightward. This raises equilibrium price and quantity. Population growth, immigration, and demographic trends are common real-world drivers of this demand shift.

facts.correctAnswer: Consumption possibilities expand beyond the PPC
acceptableAlternatives: Consumption outside the PPC becomes possible
distractors: Both countries produce more of everything
facts.quizQuestion: When two countries specialize and exchange goods with each other, what happens to their this possibilities relative to their production frontier?
facts.explanation: Specialization and trade allow countries to consume combinations of goods that lie outside their own PPC — they can consume beyond their domestic production frontier. By exporting the good they produce cheaply and importing what others produce cheaply, both countries effectively access a larger set of consumption possibilities. This is one of the most powerful insights in economics: trade makes both parties richer.

facts.quizQuestion: If consumer incomes rise and a good is classified as an inferior good (such as instant noodles or used clothing), in which direction does demand for that good shift?
facts.explanation: For inferior goods, income and demand move in opposite directions: when income rises, demand falls (shifts left). As people earn more, they switch to preferred substitutes — fewer ramen packets, fewer bus rides. This is why inferior goods have a negative income elasticity. Correctly identifying good type (normal vs. inferior) is critical for AP demand-shift questions.

facts.quizQuestion: When the government provides a per-unit subsidy to producers, effectively reducing their cost of production, in which direction does the supply curve shift?
facts.explanation: A subsidy is a government payment to producers that lowers their effective production cost. With lower costs, producers are willing to supply more at every price — the supply curve shifts rightward (increases). This lowers equilibrium price and raises equilibrium quantity. Subsidies are a key ROTTEN supply shifter (the 'T' for Taxes/subsidies).

facts.quizQuestion: If consumer tastes shift away from a good (demand decreases, curve shifts left) while supply remains unchanged, what happens to equilibrium price and the output level?
facts.explanation: A leftward demand shift (demand decreases) with supply held constant creates a surplus at the old price. The surplus drives price down, which also reduces quantity supplied as firms produce less at the lower price. The new equilibrium has both lower price and lower quantity — the standard outcome of a demand decrease.

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the price at which the market is in equilibrium — the price that eliminates both surplus and shortage simultaneously?
facts.explanation: The market-clearing price is synonymous with the equilibrium price — the price at which quantity supplied exactly equals quantity demanded. At this price, the market 'clears': every unit offered for sale finds a willing buyer and every willing buyer finds a willing seller. There is no tendency for price to change unless external factors shift supply or demand.

facts.quizQuestion: When an economy uses all available resources and technology to produce goods at its maximum output capacity, it achieves what term?
facts.explanation: Productive efficiency means producing goods at the lowest possible cost — operating on the PPC rather than inside it. Every point on the PPC is productively efficient. However, productive efficiency doesn't guarantee allocative efficiency (producing the right mix of goods for society's wants). Both types of efficiency are tested on the AP exam.

facts.quizQuestion: Which economic principle states that, all else equal, when the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded falls?
facts.explanation: The law of demand establishes an inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded. As price increases, consumers buy less; as price decreases, consumers buy more, assuming all other factors remain constant.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name for a table showing the quantity of a good a consumer is willing and able to buy at each possible price?
facts.explanation: A demand schedule lists price-quantity pairs for a buyer. Plotting these points on a graph creates the demand curve. Each row shows how much would be purchased at a given price, reflecting the law of demand.

facts.quizQuestion: To derive market demand from individual demand curves, economists use which method?
facts.explanation: Market demand is found by horizontal summation — at each price level, you add up the quantities demanded by all individual consumers. This produces a market demand curve that lies to the right of any single individual's demand.

facts.quizQuestion: Which economic principle states that, all else equal, a higher price for a good leads producers to supply a greater quantity?
facts.explanation: The law of supply describes a positive relationship between price and quantity supplied. Higher prices make production more profitable, motivating sellers to increase output. This is why supply curves slope upward.

facts.quizQuestion: What is a table called that shows the quantity of a good a producer is willing and able to supply at each possible price?
facts.explanation: A supply schedule pairs prices with the quantities a producer will offer for sale at each price. It reflects the law of supply — as price rises, the quantities listed increase. Graphing these points produces the supply curve.

facts.quizQuestion: What measure calculates the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price?
facts.explanation: Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures how responsive consumers are to price changes. A PED of -2 means a 1% price increase causes a 2% decrease in quantity demanded. The absolute value is typically used for comparison.

facts.quizQuestion: Which this calculates elasticity using average values for price and quantity as the base, ensuring the same result regardless of which direction the price change goes?
facts.explanation: The midpoint formula computes elasticity as (change in Q / average Q) divided by (change in P / average P). Using averages as denominators eliminates directionality bias, giving the same elasticity whether price rises or falls between two points.

facts.quizQuestion: When the absolute value of price elasticity of demand is greater than 1, demand is classified as which type?
facts.explanation: Elastic demand (|PED| > 1) means consumers are highly responsive to price changes. A 1% price increase causes more than a 1% drop in quantity demanded. Luxury goods and items with many substitutes typically have elastic demand.

facts.quizQuestion: When the absolute value of price elasticity of demand is between 0 and 1, demand is classified as which type?
facts.explanation: Inelastic demand (0 < |PED| < 1) means consumers are relatively unresponsive to price changes. A 1% price increase causes less than a 1% drop in quantity demanded. Necessities like insulin and gasoline typically exhibit inelastic demand.

facts.quizQuestion: When the absolute value of price elasticity of demand equals exactly 1, what is demand called?
facts.explanation: Unit elastic demand (|PED| = 1) means the percentage change in quantity demanded exactly equals the percentage change in price. Total revenue remains unchanged when price changes along a unit elastic demand curve.

facts.quizQuestion: A horizontal demand curve represents which extreme type of price elasticity of demand?
facts.explanation: Perfectly elastic demand (PED = infinity) is shown as a horizontal demand curve. Consumers will buy any quantity at the going price but will buy nothing at even a slightly higher price. This occurs in perfectly competitive markets where many identical substitutes exist.

facts.quizQuestion: A vertical demand curve represents which extreme type of price elasticity of demand where consumers buy the same quantity regardless of price?
facts.explanation: Perfectly inelastic demand (PED = 0) is shown as a vertical demand curve. Consumers buy the exact same quantity no matter what price is charged. Life-saving medications with no substitutes are often cited as approaching this extreme.

facts.quizQuestion: Which determinant of price elasticity of demand is considered the most important, as it determines how easily consumers can switch away from a good when its price rises?
facts.explanation: The availability of substitutes is the primary determinant of PED. When many close substitutes exist (e.g., one brand of cola), demand is elastic. When few substitutes exist (e.g., a specific prescription drug), demand is inelastic.

acceptableAlternatives: Whether a good is a necessity or luxury
facts.quizQuestion: Which determinant of PED explains why insulin tends to be inelastic while vacation cruises tend to be elastic?
facts.explanation: Necessities (goods people must have) tend to have inelastic demand because consumers buy them regardless of price increases. Luxuries (non-essential goods) tend to be elastic because consumers can forgo them if prices rise.

facts.quizQuestion: Which determinant of PED explains why demand for gasoline is more inelastic in the short run than in the long run?
facts.explanation: Over longer time horizons, consumers have more opportunity to adjust behavior — buying fuel-efficient cars, moving closer to work, or finding substitutes. The longer the time period, the more elastic demand tends to be.

facts.correctAnswer: Share of income spent on the good
facts.quizQuestion: Which determinant of PED explains why demand for salt is more inelastic than demand for a new car, even if both prices rise by the same percentage?
facts.explanation: Goods that take up a large share of consumer budgets (cars, rent) tend to have more elastic demand because consumers notice and react to price changes. Goods with tiny budget shares (salt, matches) tend to be inelastic.

facts.quizQuestion: When demand is elastic and a seller raises price, what happens to the total amount received from sales?
facts.explanation: With elastic demand, the percentage drop in quantity demanded exceeds the percentage price increase, so revenue falls. This is the total revenue test: for elastic demand, price and total revenue move in opposite directions.

facts.quizQuestion: When demand is inelastic and a seller raises price, what happens to the total amount received from sales?
facts.explanation: With inelastic demand, the percentage drop in quantity demanded is smaller than the percentage price increase, so revenue rises. Price and total revenue move in the same direction when demand is inelastic.

facts.quizQuestion: When demand is unit elastic and price changes, what happens to the total amount received from sales?
facts.explanation: At unit elasticity (|PED| = 1), the percentage change in quantity exactly offsets the percentage change in price, leaving total revenue (P × Q) unchanged. This is the revenue-maximizing point on a linear demand curve.

facts.correctAnswer: Slope and elasticity are not the same
distractors: Steeper slope always means more inelastic
Flatter slope always means unit elastic
Elasticity is constant along any line
Slope and elasticity are both unitless
Parallel lines have the same elasticity
facts.quizQuestion: Why is it incorrect to say a steeper demand curve is always more inelastic than a flatter one?
facts.explanation: Slope (change in P / change in Q) is a ratio of absolute changes; elasticity uses percentage changes. Two parallel demand curves have the same slope but different elasticities. Along a single linear demand curve, elasticity changes continuously from elastic to inelastic as you move down.

facts.correctAnswer: Elasticity varies along a linear demand curve
acceptableAlternatives: PED changes along a straight-line demand curve
distractors: Elasticity is constant along a linear demand curve
The midpoint of a demand curve is always inelastic
Slope determines where elasticity equals one
Perfectly elastic demand has a constant slope
The top of a demand curve is always inelastic
Unit elastic point is at the x-intercept
Elasticity equals slope at every point
facts.quizQuestion: On a straight-line (linear) downward-sloping demand curve, how does price the sensitivity coefficient of demand vary as you move from the top to the bottom of the curve?
facts.explanation: At the top of a linear demand curve (high price, low quantity), demand is elastic. At the midpoint, it is unit elastic. At the bottom (low price, high quantity), demand is inelastic. This is because the percentage changes in P and Q differ at each point even though the absolute slope stays the same.

facts.quizQuestion: What the sensitivity coefficient measure calculates the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in consumer income?
facts.explanation: Income elasticity of demand (YED) measures how responsive demand is to changes in income. A positive YED indicates a normal good; a negative YED indicates an inferior good. YED greater than 1 identifies luxury goods.

facts.quizQuestion: A positive income elasticity of demand (YED > 0) indicates that a good is which type?
facts.explanation: When YED is positive, demand rises as income rises, meaning the good is a normal good. Consumers buy more of it when they have more money. Examples include restaurant meals, clothing, and electronics.

facts.quizQuestion: A negative income elasticity of demand (YED < 0) identifies a as which type, meaning demand falls as income rises?
facts.explanation: Inferior goods have YED < 0: as income increases, consumers buy less of them and switch to higher-quality alternatives. Examples include instant noodles, bus rides, and generic store brands.

facts.quizQuestion: A good with income elasticity of demand greater than 1 (YED > 1) is classified as which type, indicating demand grows faster than income?
facts.explanation: Luxury (superior) goods have YED > 1, meaning a 1% income increase causes more than a 1% increase in demand. As societies grow wealthier, spending on luxuries like designer goods, yachts, and fine dining rises disproportionately.

facts.quizQuestion: A good with income elasticity between 0 and 1 (0 < YED < 1) is classified as which type, indicating demand rises with income but less than proportionally?
facts.explanation: Necessities have YED between 0 and 1: demand grows as income rises, but more slowly than income itself. Food staples, utilities, and basic clothing are typical examples — people need them but don't dramatically increase consumption when they earn more.

facts.quizQuestion: What the sensitivity coefficient measure calculates the percentage change in quantity demanded of Good A divided by the percentage change in the price of Good B?
facts.explanation: Cross-price elasticity of demand (XED) measures how the demand for one good responds to price changes in another. A positive XED indicates substitutes; a negative XED indicates complements.

facts.quizQuestion: A positive cross-price elasticity of demand (XED > 0) between two items indicates they are which type, where buying more of one reduces demand for the other?
facts.explanation: When XED is positive, a rise in the price of Good B increases demand for Good A — consumers switch from B to A because they serve similar purposes. Butter and margarine, Coke and Pepsi, and coffee and tea are classic substitutes.

facts.quizQuestion: A negative cross-price elasticity of demand (XED < 0) between two items indicates they are which type, used together such that a rise in the price of one reduces demand for the other?
facts.explanation: When XED is negative, a rise in the price of Good B decreases demand for Good A — the goods are used together. When gas prices rise and driving becomes more expensive, demand for cars also falls. Printers and ink cartridges are another example.

facts.correctAnswer: Strength of the relationship between the goods
acceptableAlternatives: How closely related the two goods are
distractors: Whether the goods are normal or inferior
The absolute prices of the goods
Whether the goods are public or private
The production cost of the goods
The market share of each good
facts.quizQuestion: The magnitude (absolute value) of cross-price elasticity of demand indicates what about the relationship between two this?
facts.explanation: A larger absolute XED means the goods are more closely related — stronger substitutes or stronger complements. For example, Coke and Pepsi (close substitutes) have a higher XED than Coke and orange juice (weaker substitutes).

facts.quizQuestion: What measure calculates the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price?
facts.explanation: Price elasticity of supply (PES) measures how responsive producers are to price changes. A PES of 2 means a 1% price increase causes a 2% increase in quantity supplied. PES is always positive because supply curves slope upward.

facts.quizQuestion: When price elasticity of supply is greater than 1 (PES > 1), supply is classified as which type?
facts.explanation: Elastic supply (PES > 1) means producers respond strongly to price changes. A 1% price increase causes more than a 1% increase in quantity supplied. Industries with flexible production processes and available inputs tend to have elastic supply.

facts.quizQuestion: When price elasticity of supply is between 0 and 1 (0 < PES < 1), supply is classified as which type?
facts.explanation: Inelastic supply (0 < PES < 1) means producers respond weakly to price changes. A 1% price increase causes less than a 1% increase in quantity supplied. Industries with fixed capacity, specialized inputs, or long production times tend to have inelastic supply.

distractors: Number of buyers in the market
facts.quizQuestion: Which determinant of PES explains why the supply of wine is more inelastic than the supply of bottled water?
facts.explanation: Goods that take longer to produce have more inelastic supply because producers cannot quickly increase output. Wine requires years of grape cultivation; bottled water can be scaled up in weeks. Longer production times mean lower PES.

facts.quizQuestion: A factory operating at only 60% utilization will have more elastic supply than a factory running at full utilization because of which supply determinant?
facts.explanation: Spare productive capacity allows producers to ramp up output quickly when prices rise. A firm running at 60% capacity can boost production immediately; a firm at full capacity must invest in new equipment first, making supply more inelastic.

facts.quizQuestion: Producers of canned goods have more elastic supply than flower growers because of which determinant of PES?
facts.explanation: Goods that can be stored in inventory allow suppliers to increase quantity supplied quickly by releasing stock. Perishable goods like fresh flowers cannot be stockpiled, so supply responds more slowly to price increases.

facts.quizQuestion: Which determinant of PES explains why a software firm can expand output more easily than a diamond mine when prices rise?
facts.explanation: When inputs (labor, materials, land) are readily available, producers can scale up quickly, making supply more elastic. Diamond mining requires rare geological formations and specialized equipment, limiting input availability and making supply inelastic.

facts.correctAnswer: More elastic in the long run
acceptableAlternatives: Supply becomes more elastic over time
distractors: More elastic in the short run
More inelastic in the long run
Perfectly inelastic in the long run
Perfectly elastic in the short run
facts.quizQuestion: Compared to the short run, how does price elasticity of supply generally change over the long run?
facts.explanation: In the long run, producers have time to build new factories, train workers, develop new inputs, and enter the market. This greater flexibility means supply becomes more elastic. In the short run, production capacity is fixed.

facts.quizQuestion: A horizontal supply curve represents which extreme type of price elasticity of supply?
facts.explanation: Perfectly elastic supply (PES = infinity) is shown as a horizontal supply curve. Producers will supply any quantity at the going price but will supply nothing below it. This occurs in constant-cost industries with unlimited access to inputs at a fixed cost.

facts.quizQuestion: A vertical supply curve represents which extreme type of price elasticity of supply where the quantity offered cannot change regardless of price?
facts.explanation: Perfectly inelastic supply (PES = 0) is shown as a vertical supply curve. The quantity is fixed regardless of price. Land in a specific location and original paintings by a deceased artist are classic examples.

facts.correctAnswer: The more inelastic side bears more of the tax
acceptableAlternatives: Inelastic party bears greater tax burden
Less elastic side pays more tax
distractors: The more elastic side bears more of the tax
The government always splits the tax equally
The legal payer always bears the full burden
Sellers always bear the full burden of a tax
Buyers always bear the full burden of a tax
Tax burden is unrelated to elasticity
Both sides always bear equal shares
facts.quizQuestion: What is the general rule for how an excise tax is distributed between buyers and sellers based on elasticity?
facts.explanation: Tax incidence falls more heavily on whichever side of the market is less able to avoid the tax — the more inelastic side. An inelastic buyer cannot easily reduce purchases when price rises; an inelastic seller cannot easily cut production when price received falls.

facts.correctAnswer: The more elastic side can escape more of the tax
acceptableAlternatives: Elastic party shifts more of tax burden away
distractors: The more elastic side bears more of the tax
Elastic sellers always pass all taxes to buyers
Elastic buyers always absorb the full tax
The government collects more from elastic markets
Elastic parties cannot reduce their tax burden
Both sides share equally regardless of elasticity
Tax incidence only matters for income taxes
facts.quizQuestion: When demand is elastic and supply is inelastic, which party bears the larger share of an excise tax?
facts.explanation: When demand is elastic, buyers can easily reduce purchases or switch to alternatives if price rises, so sellers (whose supply is inelastic and cannot cut production easily) end up absorbing most of the tax burden.

facts.correctAnswer: Consumers (buyers) bear more of the tax
acceptableAlternatives: Smokers pay most of the cigarette tax
distractors: Producers (sellers) bear more of the tax
Government bears most of the tax
Both sides bear exactly equal shares
Tobacco companies bear all the burden
facts.quizQuestion: Cigarette demand is highly inelastic while tobacco supply is relatively elastic. When an excise tax is imposed on cigarettes, which party bears the larger tax burden?
facts.explanation: Because demand for cigarettes is inelastic (addictive, few substitutes) while supply is elastic (tobacco can be grown elsewhere), buyers cannot easily reduce consumption and end up bearing most of the tax as higher retail prices.

facts.correctAnswer: % change in Qd divided by % change in P
acceptableAlternatives: Percentage change in quantity demanded over percentage change in price
distractors: % change in P divided by % change in Qd
% change in Qs divided by % change in P
Change in Qd divided by change in P
Change in P divided by change in Qd
% change in income divided by % change in Qd
Change in TR divided by change in P
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for calculating price elasticity of demand?
facts.explanation: PED = (% change in quantity demanded) / (% change in price). Because demand curves slope downward, PED is typically negative, but economists usually report the absolute value. The midpoint formula uses average values to avoid directionality issues.

facts.correctAnswer: Tax is shared between buyers and sellers
acceptableAlternatives: Both sides bear part of the tax burden
Burden split between consumers and producers
distractors: Sellers bear the entire tax burden
Buyers bear the entire tax burden
The government bears the tax burden
Only the party legally taxed bears the burden
The tax eliminates the market entirely
Tax incidence depends only on who writes the check
The burden falls entirely on the most profitable firm
facts.quizQuestion: When both demand and supply are relatively inelastic, how is the burden of an excise tax distributed?
facts.explanation: When both sides are inelastic, neither buyers nor sellers can easily adjust quantities. The tax burden is shared — buyers pay higher prices and sellers receive lower net revenue. The more inelastic side still bears more, but neither side fully escapes.

distractors: Most elastic point on the curve
Most inelastic point on the curve
The x-intercept of the demand curve
The y-intercept of the demand curve
facts.quizQuestion: A revenue-maximizing seller operating on a linear demand curve will set price at which elasticity point?
facts.explanation: Total revenue is maximized at the unit elastic point (|PED| = 1), which is the midpoint of a linear demand curve. Above this point demand is elastic and lowering price raises revenue; below it demand is inelastic and raising price increases revenue.

facts.quizQuestion: A tax levied on each unit of a specific good sold — such as a per-gallon tax on gasoline — is called what?
facts.explanation: An excise tax (per-unit tax) is charged on each unit of a good produced or sold. It drives a wedge between the price buyers pay and the price sellers receive. The distribution of this tax wedge between buyers and sellers depends on relative elasticities.

facts.correctAnswer: Lower the price to increase total revenue
distractors: Raise the price to increase total revenue
Keep the price unchanged to maximize revenue
Raise the price to maximize profit margin
Lower the price to reduce quantity sold
Raise the price to reduce consumer surplus
Offer discounts only during peak demand
facts.quizQuestion: A firm facing elastic demand for its product should do what to its price in order to upward change total revenue?
facts.explanation: With elastic demand, lowering price increases quantity demanded by a greater percentage than the price decrease, so TR rises. This is why airlines, streaming services, and software firms often use price cuts and promotions to grow revenue.

facts.correctAnswer: Raise the price to increase total revenue
distractors: Lower the price to increase total revenue
Keep the price unchanged to maximize revenue
Lower the price to attract new buyers
Offer volume discounts to boost quantity
Reduce price to improve market share
Lower price to maximize consumer surplus
facts.quizQuestion: A firm facing inelastic demand for its product should do what to its price in order to upward change total revenue?
facts.explanation: With inelastic demand, raising price causes a smaller proportional drop in quantity demanded, so TR rises. Drug companies and utility providers often raise prices when demand is inelastic, knowing customers have few alternatives.

facts.correctAnswer: Narrowly defined markets have more elastic demand
acceptableAlternatives: Narrower market definitions yield higher PED
Specific goods are more elastic than broad categories
distractors: Broadly defined markets have more elastic demand
Market breadth does not affect elasticity
Narrow markets always have inelastic demand
