facts.quizQuestion: Why does demand for 'beef' tend to be more elastic than demand for 'food in general'?
facts.explanation: Narrowly defined goods have more substitutes available — beef competes with chicken, pork, and fish. Broadly defined categories like 'food' have no close substitutes at all. The more substitutes available, the higher the PED.

facts.quizQuestion: The area below the demand curve and above the market price represents what welfare concept?
facts.explanation: Consumer surplus measures the benefit buyers receive beyond what they actually pay. Graphically, it is the triangular area above the price level and below the demand curve.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The area above the supply curve and below the market price represents what welfare concept?
facts.explanation: Producer surplus measures the benefit sellers receive beyond the minimum price they would have accepted. It equals the triangular area below the price level and above the supply curve.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: Consumer welfare gain plus producer welfare gain equals what combined efficiency measure used in welfare economics?
facts.explanation: Total surplus (also called social surplus) is the sum of consumer and producer surplus, representing the total net benefit to society from market transactions.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: When resources are allocated so that marginal benefit equals marginal cost (P = MC), the market achieves what type of efficiency?
facts.explanation: Allocative efficiency occurs when resources flow to their highest-valued uses, achieved when price equals marginal cost. At this point, total surplus is maximized and no reallocation can make someone better off without harming another.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: At a competitive market equilibrium where P = MC, total surplus is ___.
facts.explanation: A competitive equilibrium achieves allocative efficiency because all mutually beneficial trades occur and none that create losses. Total surplus reaches its theoretical maximum at the intersection of supply and demand.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The loss of total surplus caused by a market distortion such as a tax or price control is called what?
facts.explanation: Deadweight loss (DWL) represents the reduction in total surplus from trades that would have been mutually beneficial but do not occur due to a market distortion. It is the triangular area between the supply and demand curves outside the distorted quantity.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A government-set legal maximum price that sellers may charge for a good is called a ___.
facts.explanation: A price ceiling prevents prices from rising above the set level. It is binding (has real market effects) only when the ceiling is set below the free-market equilibrium price, creating a shortage.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A price ceiling is binding (creates market effects) only when it is set ___ the free-market equilibrium price.
facts.explanation: A binding price ceiling is one set below equilibrium. Since the legal maximum is lower than where supply and demand balance, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, resulting in a persistent shortage.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A binding price ceiling causes quantity demanded to exceed quantity supplied, creating a market ___.
facts.explanation: Because the ceiling prevents the price from rising to clear the market, more consumers want the good than producers are willing to supply at the controlled price. This gap is a shortage (excess demand).
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: Which real-world policy is the classic AP Microeconomics example of a binding price ceiling?
facts.explanation: Rent control caps the price landlords may charge for housing. Because it is typically set below the market-clearing rent, it creates a housing shortage, reduces maintenance incentives, and generates deadweight loss.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A government-set legal minimum price below which a good cannot be sold is called a ___.
facts.explanation: A price floor prevents the market price from falling below the set level. It is binding only when the floor is set above the equilibrium price, creating a surplus (excess supply).
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A price floor is binding (creates market effects) only when it is set ___ the free-market equilibrium price.
facts.explanation: A binding price floor is above the equilibrium price. The artificially high minimum price encourages more production than consumers want at that price, creating a persistent surplus (excess supply).
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A binding price floor causes quantity supplied to exceed quantity demanded, creating a market ___.
facts.explanation: The above-equilibrium floor price encourages more production but discourages consumption, so sellers cannot find buyers for all they produce. This gap between quantity supplied and quantity demanded is a surplus.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: Which real-world policy is the classic AP Microeconomics example of a binding price floor?
facts.explanation: The minimum wage sets a legal floor on wages. When it is above the equilibrium wage, it creates unemployment (a labor surplus) because the quantity of labor supplied exceeds the quantity demanded at that wage.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A price ceiling set above the equilibrium price or a price floor set below equilibrium has ___ on the market.
facts.explanation: Non-binding price controls do not constrain the market because the equilibrium price already satisfies the legal limit. The market naturally settles at equilibrium, so the control is irrelevant.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: Binding price ceilings can encourage illegal trade at prices above the ceiling, known as a ___.
facts.explanation: When a legal price ceiling prevents the market from clearing, unsatisfied buyers are willing to pay more and some sellers will transact illegally above the ceiling price. This underground trading is called a black market.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A fixed dollar amount collected for each this of a good sold, which shifts the supply curve upward, is called a ___.
facts.explanation: A per-unit (excise) tax adds a constant amount to the cost of each unit produced. Sellers effectively face a higher cost schedule, shifting the supply curve up (left) by exactly the tax amount.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A per-unit tax on sellers shifts the supply curve ___ by the amount of the tax.
facts.explanation: Because the tax raises the effective cost of production for each unit, sellers require a higher price at every quantity. This is represented graphically as a leftward (upward) parallel shift of the supply curve by the tax amount.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The difference between the price buyers pay and the price sellers receive after a per-unit tax is called the ___.
facts.explanation: The tax wedge equals the per-unit tax amount. Buyers pay a higher price than before the tax, sellers receive a lower net price, and the government collects the difference per unit sold.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The distribution of a tax's economic burden between buyers and sellers is called ___.
facts.explanation: Tax incidence describes who actually bears the cost of a tax, which may differ from who legally pays it. The split depends on the relative elasticities of supply and demand.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

distractors: The side that legally pays the tax
The side with more market power
facts.quizQuestion: When a tax is imposed on a market, the ___ bears a greater share of the tax burden.
facts.explanation: The party with less ability to adjust quantity (lower price elasticity) cannot escape the tax by changing behavior, so they absorb a larger share of the burden. For example, if demand is very inelastic, buyers end up paying most of the tax.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A per-unit tax reduces quantity traded below the efficient level, causing ___ in addition to the revenue transfer to government.
facts.explanation: The tax raises the price buyers pay and lowers the net price sellers receive, reducing the quantity exchanged. The transactions that would have occurred without the tax but no longer do represent lost total surplus, measured as the deadweight loss triangle.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A government payment to producers that lowers their costs of production and shifts supply rightward (downward) is called a ___.
facts.explanation: A per-unit subsidy lowers producers' effective costs, shifting the supply curve down (right) by the subsidy amount. This increases equilibrium quantity and lowers the price consumers pay, though the government bears the cost.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A per-unit subsidy paid to sellers shifts the supply curve ___ by the amount of the subsidy.
facts.explanation: The subsidy reduces the effective cost per unit, so sellers are willing to supply more at every price. This is shown graphically as a rightward (downward) parallel shift of the supply curve by the subsidy amount.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: Although subsidies increase quantity traded beyond equilibrium and lower consumer prices, they also create ___ because the social cost exceeds social benefit at those extra units.
facts.explanation: A subsidy pushes output beyond the allocatively efficient level where MB = MC. The extra units produced cost society more (in subsidy payments) than consumers value them, generating a deadweight loss triangle on the supply side of the market.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: In trade models, the prevailing price on international markets that a small open economy takes as given is called the ___.
facts.explanation: A small economy is a price taker on world markets: its own production and consumption decisions do not affect the world price. This price then determines whether the country imports or exports the good.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A country ___ a good when the world price is lower than its domestic no-trade equilibrium price.
facts.explanation: If the world price is below the domestic autarky price, domestic consumers can buy more cheaply abroad. The country buys the difference between domestic quantity demanded and domestic quantity supplied from foreign producers, meaning it imports.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A country ___ a good when the world price is higher than its domestic no-trade equilibrium price.
facts.explanation: If the world price exceeds the autarky price, domestic producers can sell profitably on world markets. The country sells the excess of domestic quantity supplied over domestic quantity demanded to foreign buyers, meaning it exports.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A tax levied on imported goods that raises the domestic price above the world price is called a ___.
facts.explanation: A tariff makes imported goods more expensive, protecting domestic producers but harming consumers. It raises the domestic price, reducing imports, while generating government revenue equal to the tariff rate times the quantity imported.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: A legal limit on the quantity of a foreign good that may be imported is called an ___.
facts.explanation: An import quota restricts the physical volume of imports, raising the domestic price similar to a tariff. Unlike a tariff, the government collects no revenue; instead, the price difference (quota rent) goes to whoever holds the import licenses.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: When a tariff raises the domestic price above the world price, which groups win and forfeit among domestic buyers and sellers?
facts.explanation: The higher domestic price transfers surplus from consumers (who pay more) to producers (who receive more). The government also gains tariff revenue, but overall there is a net deadweight loss because fewer mutually beneficial trades occur.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

acceptableAlternatives: quota rent goes to license holders
distractors: Generates government revenue like a tariff
facts.quizQuestion: Unlike a tariff, an import quota that raises the domestic price produces ___ for the government.
facts.explanation: A quota limits import volume directly. The resulting price increase creates quota rent (the difference between the domestic and world prices on imported units), but this rent accrues to whoever holds import licenses, not the government, unless licenses are auctioned.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: Compared to no-trade autarky, opening an economy to free trade causes ___.
facts.explanation: Free trade allows each country to buy goods at the world price, expanding the set of mutually beneficial transactions. Total surplus (consumer plus producer surplus, summed across countries) rises, even though some domestic groups may be harmed.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The rate at which a country exchanges its exports for imports from other countries is called the ___.
facts.explanation: The terms of trade measure the relative price of a country's exports in terms of its imports. Favorable terms of trade (rising export prices or falling import prices) allow a country to obtain more imports per unit of exports, increasing national welfare.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

distractors: Marginal cost and marginal revenue curves
Average total cost and average revenue curves
Total revenue and total cost curves
facts.quizQuestion: Consumer and producer surplus are measured as areas in a graph containing the ___.
facts.explanation: Consumer surplus is the area under the demand curve and above the price; producer surplus is the area above the supply curve and below the price. Both are computed using the supply and demand diagram.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: On a supply-and-demand graph, a tax or price control creates a ___ between the curves, representing lost total surplus.
facts.explanation: The deadweight loss triangle is bounded by the supply curve, demand curve, and the distorted quantity. It represents transactions that would have occurred in a free market (where MB > MC) but are prevented by the market distortion.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

distractors: Whoever legally pays the tax bears the full burden
The government bears part of the burden
The more elastic side bears more
The side with more suppliers bears more
facts.quizQuestion: Which principle explains why a cigarette tax (very inelastic demand) falls mostly on buyers?
facts.explanation: When demand is inelastic, buyers cannot easily reduce quantity demanded in response to higher prices, so they absorb most of the tax. The general rule is that the more inelastic side bears the greater share of the tax burden.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: Because a binding price ceiling creates a shortage, some mechanism other than price must allocate the scarce good, a process called ___.
facts.explanation: When price is legally prevented from rising to clear the market, alternative allocation mechanisms emerge: waiting in queues, first-come first-served, coupons, favoritism, or government allocation. These are all forms of non-price rationing.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: To maintain a binding price floor when there is excess supply, a government may resort to ___ to absorb the unsold quantity.
facts.explanation: If a price floor creates a surplus (like in agricultural support programs), the government sometimes must purchase the excess supply itself to keep the market price from falling below the floor. This adds to government expenditures.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

acceptableAlternatives: consumers gain and producers lose from imports
distractors: The country always loses from imports
facts.quizQuestion: When a country opens to free trade and imports at the world price (below autarky price), which domestic groups win and forfeit?
facts.explanation: The lower world price raises consumer surplus (buyers pay less) but reduces producer surplus (sellers receive less). Total domestic welfare still rises because the consumer gain exceeds the producer loss, but import-competing producers and their workers are harmed.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

acceptableAlternatives: producers gain and consumers lose from exports
distractors: The country always gains from exports
facts.quizQuestion: When a country opens to free trade and exports at the world price (above autarky price), which domestic groups win and forfeit?
facts.explanation: The higher world price raises producer surplus (sellers receive more) but reduces consumer surplus (buyers pay more). Total domestic welfare still rises because the producer gain exceeds the consumer loss, but domestic consumers are harmed.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The domestic equilibrium price that would prevail if a country did not trade internationally is called the ___.
facts.explanation: The autarky price is where domestic supply equals domestic demand in isolation. Comparing it to the world price determines whether a country would import (world P < autarky P) or export (world P > autarky P) when trade opens.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.correctAnswer: Reduces imports and raises domestic price
distractors: Increases imports and lowers domestic price
Raises imports and raises domestic price
Reduces imports and lowers domestic price
Has no effect on import volume
facts.quizQuestion: The primary market effect of a tariff on this is that it ___ compared to free trade.
facts.explanation: A tariff raises the effective price of imports, making foreign goods less competitive. Domestic quantity demanded falls, domestic quantity supplied rises, and the gap between them (imports) shrinks. The domestic price rises toward the autarky level.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: On a supply-and-demand diagram showing a per-unit tax, the government's tax revenue is represented by the ___ between the buyer's price and seller's net price over the quantity sold.
facts.explanation: The tax revenue equals the per-unit tax times the quantity traded after the tax. Graphically, this is a rectangle whose height is the tax wedge (buyer's price minus seller's net price) and whose width is the post-tax quantity.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The more elastic supply and demand are in a taxed market, the ___ the tax creates.
facts.explanation: Elastic supply and demand mean buyers and sellers respond strongly to the price changes caused by the tax, so the quantity reduction is large. A large quantity reduction means the deadweight loss triangle is large.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: When a per-unit tax is imposed on a competitive market, what happens to consumer the excess quantity?
facts.explanation: The tax raises the price buyers pay, shrinking the area above the price and below the demand curve. Consumer surplus unambiguously decreases as a result of any tax that raises the consumer price.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: When a per-unit tax is imposed on a competitive market, what happens to this surplus?
facts.explanation: The tax lowers the net price sellers receive, shrinking the area above the supply curve and below the price. Producer surplus unambiguously decreases because sellers retain less from each unit sold.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: In a surplus graph, consumer surplus is the triangular area above the price level and below the ___.
facts.explanation: The demand curve shows the maximum price each consumer is willing to pay (marginal benefit). Consumer surplus is the difference between what buyers are willing to pay (on the demand curve) and what they actually pay (the market price).
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: In a surplus graph, producer surplus is the triangular area below the price level and above the ___.
facts.explanation: The supply curve shows the minimum price each producer is willing to accept (marginal cost). Producer surplus is the difference between the market price and the minimum price producers would have accepted.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.quizQuestion: The gain captured by holders of import licenses when a this raises the domestic price above the world price is called ___.
facts.explanation: An import quota creates scarcity that drives up the domestic price. License holders buy imports at the world price and sell domestically at the higher quota-inclusive price, pocketing the difference per unit. This rent-like gain is the quota rent.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 2

facts.correctAnswer: At least one input is fixed
distractors: The firm has no fixed costs
facts.quizQuestion: In economics, what defines the short run for a firm?
facts.explanation: The short run is the production period in which at least one input — typically capital like a factory or equipment — cannot be changed. Only variable inputs like labor can be adjusted. The long run is the period in which all inputs become variable.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: period where all inputs can change
distractors: At least one input is fixed
facts.quizQuestion: What distinguishes the long run from the short run in production theory?
facts.explanation: In the long run, a firm has enough time to adjust all inputs, including capital such as factory size or equipment. Because no input is fixed, there are no fixed costs in the long run — every cost becomes variable.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Total output produced by all workers
facts.quizQuestion: What does Total Product (TP) measure in the short-run production function?
facts.explanation: Total Product is the total quantity of output produced when a given number of workers are employed, holding other inputs fixed. As more labor is added, TP typically rises at first rapidly, then more slowly, and eventually may decline.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: change in total product divided by change in labor
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Marginal Product (MP) of labor?
facts.explanation: Marginal Product equals the change in Total Product divided by the change in labor (ΔTP/ΔL). It tells us how much additional output is gained by hiring one more worker. MP rises initially then falls due to diminishing returns.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Average Product (AP) of labor?
facts.explanation: Average Product equals Total Product divided by the quantity of labor (TP/L). It represents output per worker. The AP curve is hill-shaped — it rises when MP is above it and falls when MP is below it.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

distractors: Principle of Diminishing Returns to Scale
facts.quizQuestion: What law states that adding more of a variable input to a fixed input eventually causes MP to fall?
facts.explanation: The Law of Diminishing Marginal Returns states that as more units of a variable input (like labor) are added to a fixed input (like a factory), the marginal product of the variable input will eventually decrease. This is why the MP curve is hump-shaped.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: When Marginal Product (MP) is greater than Average Product (AP), what is happening to AP?
facts.explanation: When MP exceeds AP, each new worker contributes more than the current average, so the average is pulled upward. This is the classic 'star student' logic: if the newest test score is above the class average, the average must rise.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: At the point where the Marginal Product curve intersects the Average Product curve, what is true about AP?
facts.explanation: MP intersects AP at AP's maximum. Below that point MP > AP (pulling AP up); above that point MP < AP (pulling AP down). This intersection rule applies identically to MC crossing ATC and AVC at their minimum points.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cost does NOT change as a firm increases or decreases its output in the short run?
facts.explanation: Fixed costs are costs that remain constant regardless of how much a firm produces, including rent, insurance, and loan payments on equipment. Even if output falls to zero, fixed costs must still be paid in the short run.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cost changes directly with the level of output a firm produces?
facts.explanation: Variable costs change as output changes and include inputs like raw materials, hourly wages, and utilities. At zero output, variable cost is zero. As production rises, variable costs rise — first at a decreasing rate, then at an increasing rate due to diminishing returns.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: total cost equals fixed cost plus variable cost
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Total Cost (TC) in the short run?
facts.explanation: Total Cost is the sum of Fixed Cost and Variable Cost: TC = FC + VC. Because FC is constant, the TC curve is simply the VC curve shifted upward by the amount of fixed cost. The shape of TC mirrors the shape of VC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: average fixed cost equals FC over Q
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Average Fixed Cost (AFC)?
facts.explanation: Average Fixed Cost equals Fixed Cost divided by output (FC/Q). Because FC never changes, AFC falls continuously as Q increases — this is called 'spreading the overhead.' The AFC curve is a downward-sloping hyperbola that never touches zero.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the shape of the Average Fixed Cost (AFC) curve as output increases?
facts.explanation: AFC always falls as output increases because the fixed cost is spread over more and more units. The AFC curve is a downward-sloping hyperbola that approaches but never reaches zero — it never becomes zero because FC is always positive.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: average variable cost equals VC over Q
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Average Variable Cost (AVC)?
facts.explanation: Average Variable Cost equals Variable Cost divided by output (VC/Q). The AVC curve is U-shaped: it falls initially as workers can specialize and cooperate, then rises as diminishing marginal returns set in. MC intersects AVC at its minimum point.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Average Total Cost (ATC)?
facts.explanation: Average Total Cost equals Total Cost divided by quantity (TC/Q), or equivalently AFC plus AVC. The ATC curve is U-shaped and lies above the AVC curve, with the gap between them equal to AFC. MC intersects ATC at ATC's minimum.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: change in total cost divided by change in output
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Marginal Cost (MC)?
facts.explanation: Marginal Cost equals the change in Total Cost from producing one more unit: MC = ΔTC/ΔQ. Because FC does not change, MC also equals ΔVC/ΔQ. The MC curve is U-shaped and intersects both AVC and ATC at their minimum points.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the shape of the Marginal Cost (MC) curve?
facts.explanation: The MC curve is U-shaped. It initially falls as workers cooperate more effectively, then rises as diminishing marginal returns cause each additional unit to cost more to produce. This mirrors the inverse relationship between MP and MC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: At what specific point does the Marginal Cost (MC) curve intersect the Average Variable Cost (AVC) curve?
facts.explanation: MC intersects AVC exactly at AVC's minimum point. When MC is below AVC, it pulls AVC down; when MC is above AVC, it pulls AVC up. This is the same mathematical relationship that applies to any marginal-average pair.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: At what specific point does the Marginal Cost (MC) curve intersect the Average Total Cost (ATC) curve?
facts.explanation: MC intersects ATC at ATC's minimum point. This is the output level where a firm achieves its lowest per-unit cost, sometimes called the 'efficient scale.' When MC < ATC, ATC is falling; when MC > ATC, ATC is rising.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: The vertical gap between the ATC curve and the AVC curve at any output level equals what?
facts.explanation: The vertical distance between ATC and AVC is always equal to AFC, because ATC = AVC + AFC. Since AFC continuously decreases as output rises, the gap between ATC and AVC narrows as production increases.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: MP and MC move in opposite directions
distractors: MP and MC are always equal
MC is the reciprocal of MP
They move in the same direction
MC is always greater than MP
facts.quizQuestion: What is the relationship between Marginal Product (MP) and Marginal Cost (MC)?
facts.explanation: MP and MC have an inverse relationship: when MP is rising, MC is falling, and when MP is falling, MC is rising. This occurs because higher worker productivity means each unit costs less to produce, and lower productivity means each unit costs more.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the shape of the Average Variable Cost (AVC) curve?
facts.explanation: The AVC curve is U-shaped. It falls initially because of increasing returns (workers specializing), then rises due to diminishing marginal returns. The MC curve passes through AVC's minimum point.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the shape of the Average Total Cost (ATC) curve in the short run?
facts.explanation: The ATC curve is U-shaped, falling first because AFC falls rapidly and then rising as increasing marginal costs outweigh the falling AFC. The efficient scale (minimum ATC) is where the MC curve intersects ATC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: long run average total cost curve
facts.quizQuestion: What curve is formed by the outer boundary (envelope) of all possible short-run ATC curves in the long run?
facts.explanation: The Long-Run Average Total Cost (LRATC) curve is the envelope of all short-run ATC curves, showing the lowest possible per-unit cost at each output level when a firm can choose its optimal plant size. It is typically U-shaped.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: When the LRATC curve is falling as output increases, the firm is experiencing what?
facts.explanation: Economies of scale occur when long-run average total cost falls as output increases, meaning large-scale production is cheaper per unit. Causes include specialization, bulk purchasing, and spreading overhead. This is the downward-sloping portion of the LRATC curve.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: When the LRATC curve is rising as output increases, the firm is experiencing what?
facts.explanation: Diseconomies of scale occur when long-run average total cost rises as output increases, meaning very large firms become less efficient per unit. Causes include managerial complexity, communication breakdown, and coordination problems that come with excessive size.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: When the LRATC curve is flat (horizontal) as output increases, what is the firm experiencing?
facts.explanation: Constant returns to scale occur when LRATC remains flat as output increases, meaning doubling all inputs exactly doubles output. Per-unit costs stay the same regardless of firm size. This is the middle range of the LRATC curve.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the smallest output level at which a firm can achieve the this long-run average total cost?
facts.explanation: Minimum Efficient Scale (MES) is the smallest output at which LRATC reaches its minimum. Firms operating below MES have higher per-unit costs than optimal. MES determines how many firms can profitably compete in an industry — industries with large MES tend toward oligopoly or monopoly.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

distractors: Accounting profit = TR - TC - opportunity cost
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for This Profit?
facts.explanation: Accounting profit equals Total Revenue minus explicit (out-of-pocket) costs. It is what shows up on a firm's income statement and ignores implicit costs such as the opportunity cost of the owner's time or capital.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Economic profit = TR - explicit costs - implicit costs
acceptableAlternatives: TR minus explicit and implicit costs
distractors: Economic profit = TR - explicit costs only
Economic profit = TR - implicit costs only
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for Economic Profit?
facts.explanation: Economic profit equals Total Revenue minus all economic costs, including both explicit (out-of-pocket) and implicit (opportunity) costs. Because it deducts more costs than accounting profit, economic profit is always less than or equal to accounting profit.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: TR covers all explicit and implicit costs
distractors: Profit sufficient to cover explicit costs only
facts.quizQuestion: In economic terms, what does it mean for a firm to earn a 'normal profit'?
facts.explanation: Normal profit means the firm earns zero economic profit — Total Revenue exactly covers all costs, including implicit opportunity costs. The firm is earning just enough to keep resources in their current use, with no incentive to enter or exit the industry.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cost involves actual monetary payments to outside suppliers or workers?
facts.explanation: Explicit costs are direct, out-of-pocket payments a firm makes for inputs: wages, rent, raw materials, utilities, and loan interest. These are the costs that appear in accounting statements. They are distinguished from implicit costs, which do not involve a cash payment.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cost represents the opportunity cost of using a firm's own resources rather than a direct cash payment?
facts.explanation: Implicit costs are the opportunity costs of resources the firm already owns — for example, the salary a business owner forgoes by running their own firm, or interest not earned on capital invested in the business. They never appear on income statements but are included in economic cost.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: Economic cost includes what two types of costs?
facts.explanation: Economic cost includes both explicit (direct monetary) costs and implicit (opportunity) costs of production. This is a broader concept than accounting cost, which only counts explicit payments. Economists use economic cost because all resources have opportunity costs.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: At least normal profit (zero economic profit)
acceptableAlternatives: economic profit at or above zero
TR covers all explicit and implicit costs
facts.quizQuestion: In the long run, what is the minimum profit condition for a firm to remain in an industry?
facts.explanation: In the long run, a firm must earn at least normal profit (zero economic profit) to justify staying in business. If economic profit is negative, the firm's resources would earn more in their next best alternative use, so rational owners exit the industry.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: When Marginal Product (MP) is less than Average Product (AP), what is happening to AP?
facts.explanation: When MP is below AP, each new worker adds less than the current average, dragging the average down. This is the 'below-average test score pulls down the class average' logic. AP falls whenever MP < AP.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: MP is zero at max TP
distractors: Average Product is at its maximum
Total Cost is at its minimum
Average Variable Cost is at its minimum
Marginal Product is at its maximum
Average Total Cost equals Marginal Cost
facts.quizQuestion: At the output level where Total Output reaches its maximum, what is true about the Marginal Output of the variable input?
facts.explanation: TP reaches its maximum when Marginal Product equals zero. Beyond this point, adding another worker would actually reduce total output (MP becomes negative), reflecting severe overcrowding of the fixed input. This is also where the TP curve has a zero slope.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: fixed costs remain even at zero output
facts.quizQuestion: If a firm produces zero output in the short run, what happens to its this costs?
facts.explanation: Fixed costs must still be paid even if a firm produces nothing in the short run. This is why they are called 'fixed' — they are contractually obligated costs like rent or loan payments that cannot be escaped without exiting the industry. Variable costs, however, would fall to zero at zero output.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Envelope of all short-run ATC curves
distractors: Average of all short-run ATC curves
A single fixed short-run ATC curve
Upper boundary of all short-run ATCs
The marginal cost curve in the long run
A horizontal line at minimum ATC
The SRATC curve with the largest plant size
facts.quizQuestion: Geometrically, the LRATC curve is described as what in relation to the family of this-run ATC curves?
facts.explanation: The LRATC is the envelope or outer boundary of all possible short-run ATC curves — it is tangent to each SRATC at the output level for which that plant size is optimal. Each point on the LRATC shows the minimum cost achievable for that output when plant size is freely chosen.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: spreading fixed costs over more units
facts.quizQuestion: The phenomenon of AFC always falling as output rises is informally called what?
facts.explanation: Spreading the overhead refers to the process by which fixed costs are divided among more and more units of output, reducing the per-unit fixed cost. A factory that costs $1,000/day has an AFC of $1 when producing 1,000 units but only $0.10 when producing 10,000.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: variable costs are zero when output is zero
distractors: Variable cost is at its maximum
facts.quizQuestion: What is the value of Variable Cost (VC) when a firm produces this output?
facts.explanation: Variable cost equals zero when output is zero because variable inputs like labor and materials are not used when nothing is being produced. TC at zero output therefore equals FC alone, which is why the TC curve starts at the level of FC on the vertical axis.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: change in variable cost over change in output
facts.quizQuestion: Because fixed costs never change, Marginal Cost can also be expressed as what formula?
facts.explanation: Since FC does not change with output, ΔFC = 0, so ΔTC = ΔVC. Therefore MC = ΔTC/ΔQ = ΔVC/ΔQ. This means MC is driven entirely by changes in variable costs, and understanding variable input productivity is the key to understanding MC behavior.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: TR exceeds all explicit and implicit costs
acceptableAlternatives: total revenue exceeds total economic cost
distractors: The firm covers its explicit costs exactly
facts.quizQuestion: What condition produces a positive economic profit for a firm?
facts.explanation: Positive economic profit occurs when Total Revenue exceeds all economic costs — both explicit and implicit. This signals that the firm is earning above the opportunity cost of its resources, which in a competitive market will attract new entrants over time.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: average total cost equals average fixed plus average variable cost
facts.quizQuestion: What is the relationship between ATC, AFC, and AVC?
facts.explanation: Average Total Cost equals Average Fixed Cost plus Average Variable Cost (ATC = AFC + AVC). This follows directly from TC = FC + VC, divided by Q. As output rises, AFC falls, so ATC gradually approaches AVC, narrowing the gap between the two curves.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: There are no fixed costs in the long run
acceptableAlternatives: all costs are variable in the long run
distractors: Fixed costs still exist in the long run
Variable costs become fixed in the long run
The long run has higher fixed costs
Fixed costs grow in the long run
facts.quizQuestion: Why are this no fixed costs in the long run?
facts.explanation: In the long run, all inputs are variable — a firm can adjust capital, labor, technology, and plant size. Because nothing is fixed, no costs are truly fixed. Every cost in the long run varies with output, which is why the LRATC concept uses a different cost framework.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: the output where ATC is lowest
facts.quizQuestion: What is the 'efficient scale' of production for a firm?
facts.explanation: The efficient scale is the output level at which Average Total Cost (ATC) is minimized — the bottom of the U-shaped ATC curve. At this quantity, MC = ATC. Producing below or above the efficient scale results in higher per-unit costs.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: TP as a function of labor and capital
distractors: Relationship between price and quantity demanded
facts.quizQuestion: What does a firm's production function describe?
facts.explanation: A production function shows the relationship between quantities of inputs used (like labor and capital) and the maximum total output that can be produced. In the short run, with capital fixed, the production function shows TP as a function of labor alone.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: When Marginal Cost (MC) is below Average Total Cost (ATC), what is happening to ATC?
facts.explanation: When MC is below ATC, each additional unit costs less than the current average, pulling ATC downward. This is the same logic as the marginal-average relationship: a below-average new value pulls the average down. ATC reaches its minimum exactly where MC = ATC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: When a firm's Total Revenue is less than its total economic costs, the firm earns what?
facts.explanation: When TR falls short of all economic costs (explicit plus implicit), the firm suffers an economic loss (negative economic profit). In the long run, this means the firm's resources could earn more elsewhere, and rational owners will eventually exit the industry.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: TC starts at the level of Fixed Cost
acceptableAlternatives: TC curve begins at FC when Q = 0
the y-intercept of TC equals FC
distractors: TC starts at the level of Variable Cost
TC starts at Average Total Cost
facts.quizQuestion: On a Total Cost graph, where does the TC curve begin when output equals zero?
facts.explanation: When Q = 0, VC = 0, so TC = FC + 0 = FC. The TC curve's y-intercept is therefore equal to Fixed Cost. The TC curve is simply the VC curve shifted upward by the constant amount of fixed cost.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Accounting profit is higher (ignores implicit costs)
acceptableAlternatives: economic profit is lower because it includes implicit costs
distractors: Accounting profit is lower because it counts more costs
Accounting profit equals economic profit plus taxes
They differ only in how fixed costs are treated
Economic profit is higher when interest rates are low
Accounting profit is zero when normal profit is earned
facts.quizQuestion: For the same firm in the same period, which is typically higher — this profit or economic profit — and why?
facts.explanation: Accounting profit is typically higher because it only subtracts explicit (out-of-pocket) costs, ignoring implicit opportunity costs. Economic profit subtracts both explicit and implicit costs, so it is always equal to or less than accounting profit for the same firm.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the most common example of the fixed input in a firm's short-run production function?
facts.explanation: Capital — such as factory size, equipment, or plant capacity — is the most typical fixed input in the short run. It cannot be adjusted quickly because acquiring or disposing of physical capital takes time. Labor, materials, and energy are usually the variable inputs.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Opportunity cost is included in economic cost
distractors: Opportunity cost is excluded from economic cost
Opportunity cost only affects accounting profit
Opportunity cost is only relevant in the long run
Opportunity cost cancels out explicit costs
Economic cost equals opportunity cost alone
facts.quizQuestion: How does implicit cost factor into the concept of economic cost?
facts.explanation: Economic cost includes opportunity cost as an implicit cost. For example, an entrepreneur using their own savings in a business foregoes interest income — that forgone income is an implicit cost that economists count, even though no cash changes hands.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

distractors: MP starts negative and becomes positive
MP is a straight downward line
MP is a straight upward line
facts.quizQuestion: What is the typical shape of the Marginal Product (MP) curve as labor increases?
facts.explanation: The MP curve is hump-shaped (inverted U): it rises initially as workers cooperate and specialize, reaches a maximum, then falls as diminishing marginal returns set in. Eventually MP can fall to zero or below if the fixed input becomes severely overcrowded.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Price is below average variable cost
distractors: TR is less than Total Cost
TR is less than Fixed Cost
TR is less than Marginal Cost
Revenue fails to cover implicit costs
facts.quizQuestion: In the short run, a firm should shut down production temporarily when what condition holds regarding price and costs?
facts.explanation: The shutdown rule states that a firm should cease production when price falls below average variable cost (P < AVC). At this point, the firm cannot even cover its variable costs, so it loses less money by shutting down and paying only fixed costs.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED — Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the profit-maximizing rule a firm uses to decide how many units to produce?
facts.explanation: A firm maximizes profit by producing the quantity where marginal revenue equals marginal cost, but only where the MC curve is rising — this ensures the firm is not in a region where producing more would still add to profit.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: P times Q (price multiplied by quantity)
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for a firm's total revenue (TR)?
facts.explanation: Total revenue is the total amount a firm receives from selling its output, calculated by multiplying the price per unit by the number of units sold. In perfect competition, price is constant, so TR rises linearly with Q.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: change in TR divided by change in Q
distractors: change in TC divided by change in Q
change in profit divided by change in Q
change in Q divided by change in TR
change in AR divided by change in Q
facts.quizQuestion: What is the formula for marginal revenue (MR)?
facts.explanation: Marginal revenue is the additional revenue gained from selling one more unit, calculated as the change in total revenue divided by the change in quantity. In perfect competition, MR equals the market price because each additional unit sells at the same price.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: TR minus TC, or (P minus ATC) times Q
facts.quizQuestion: What are the two equivalent formulas used to calculate a firm's economic profit?
facts.explanation: Economic profit equals total revenue minus total cost. This can be rewritten as (P - ATC) × Q, which shows that if price exceeds average total cost, the firm earns positive profit; if price is below ATC, the firm takes a loss.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: P greater than ATC means profit
distractors: Operating at a loss but producing
facts.quizQuestion: When price exceeds a firm's average total cost at the profit-maximizing quantity, what is the firm's outcome?
facts.explanation: When price is above ATC, the firm earns positive economic profit on each unit sold. The rectangular area between P and ATC, multiplied by Q, represents total profit on a cost diagram.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Break-even (zero economic profit, normal profit earned)
distractors: Operating at a loss but producing
facts.quizQuestion: When price exactly equals average total cost at the profit-maximizing quantity, what outcome does the firm experience?
facts.explanation: When P = ATC, the firm earns zero economic profit but still earns normal profit, meaning all implicit and explicit costs are covered. This is the long-run equilibrium condition in perfect competition.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Produce at a loss (P covers variable costs but not all fixed costs)
distractors: Shut down only if fixed costs rise
facts.quizQuestion: When AVC is below price, but price is still below ATC, what should a profit-maximizing firm do in the short run?
facts.explanation: When AVC < P < ATC, the firm is losing money but covers all variable costs and some fixed costs. Since fixed costs are sunk in the short run, the firm minimizes losses by continuing to produce rather than shutting down and losing the full fixed cost.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Shut down (P is below AVC, revenue won't cover variable costs)
acceptableAlternatives: P less than AVC means shutdown
distractors: Exit the industry in the short run
Raise price to cover variable costs
facts.quizQuestion: When price falls this a firm's average variable cost at every output level, what is the correct short-run decision?
facts.explanation: When P < AVC, each unit produced deepens the loss because revenue doesn't even cover variable costs. The firm minimizes losses by shutting down and only losing its fixed costs.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the principle that says a firm should stop producing when price falls below minimum average variable cost?
facts.explanation: The shutdown rule states that a firm should cease production in the short run when price drops below minimum AVC. At that point, even stopping production results in a smaller total loss than continuing.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Minimum point of the AVC curve
distractors: Minimum point of the ATC curve
Minimum point of the MC curve
Maximum point of the AVC curve
Minimum point of the AFC curve
facts.quizQuestion: What graph point represents the shutdown point for a competitive firm?
facts.explanation: The shutdown point is the minimum of the AVC curve. If market price falls below this point, the firm cannot cover variable costs on any unit and should shut down to minimize losses.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Fixed costs are sunk in the short run

Fixed costs always equal variable costs

facts.quizQuestion: Why does a firm continue producing even when price is below ATC but above AVC? What key fact about costs explains this?
facts.explanation: Fixed costs are sunk in the short run, meaning they must be paid regardless of whether the firm produces or not. Since fixed costs are unavoidable, the firm ignores them in the shutdown decision and only compares price to AVC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: The MC curve above the AVC curve
acceptableAlternatives: portion of MC above minimum AVC
distractors: The MC curve above minimum ATC
The supply curve above the ATC curve

facts.quizQuestion: What portion of a competitive firm's cost curves defines its short-run supply curve?
facts.explanation: A competitive firm's short-run supply curve is the portion of its MC curve that lies at or above the minimum point of the AVC curve. Below that price, the firm shuts down, so no supply is forthcoming.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: Which market structure is defined by many firms selling identical products with free entry and exit, where firms are price takers with perfect information?
facts.explanation: Perfect competition requires five conditions: many independent firms, identical (homogeneous) products, free entry and exit, price-taking behavior, and perfect information. It serves as the benchmark against which all other market structures are compared.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes a firm in perfect competition that must accept the market price and cannot influence it?
facts.explanation: A price taker is a firm so small relative to the market that its output decisions have no effect on market price. Because many identical sellers exist, any single firm that tries to charge above market price would lose all its customers.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Perfectly elastic (horizontal) at the market price
distractors: Unit elastic at the market price
facts.quizQuestion: What does the demand curve facing an individual firm in a perfectly competitive market look like?
facts.explanation: Because a competitive firm is a price taker, it can sell any quantity at the going market price but nothing above it. This creates a perfectly elastic (horizontal) demand curve for the individual firm at the market-determined price.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: P = MR = AR = D (price equals marginal revenue equals average revenue equals demand)
facts.quizQuestion: In perfect competition, what special relationship holds among price, marginal revenue, the weighted mean revenue, and demand for the individual firm?
facts.explanation: Because a perfectly competitive firm sells every unit at the same market price, each additional unit adds exactly that price to revenue (MR = P), average revenue per unit always equals price (AR = P), and the firm's demand curve is horizontal at that price (D = P). These four values are identical.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: P = MR = MC (price equals marginal revenue equals marginal cost)
acceptableAlternatives: price equals MC in short-run equilibrium
facts.quizQuestion: What three-way equality characterizes the short-run equilibrium condition for a perfectly competitive firm?
facts.explanation: In short-run competitive equilibrium, a firm produces where MR = MC (profit maximization), and since MR = P in perfect competition, this means P = MR = MC. The firm may still earn a profit, loss, or break even depending on the relationship between P and ATC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Horizontal sum of all individual firms' MC curves above AVC
acceptableAlternatives: add each firm's MC above AVC horizontally
distractors: Vertical sum of all firms' ATC curves
The average of all firms' MC curves
Sum of each firm's MR curves horizontally
Horizontal sum of all firms' ATC curves
Vertical sum of individual demand curves
Horizontal sum of firms' AFC curves
Average of individual firms' AVC curves
facts.quizQuestion: How is the short-run industry supply curve derived from a single person firms' cost curves in a perfectly competitive market?
facts.explanation: Because each firm's supply curve is its MC curve above minimum AVC, the market supply curve is obtained by horizontally summing all the individual firms' supply curves. At any given price, total industry quantity supplied is the sum of what every firm individually produces at that price.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

firms enter until economic profit disappears

facts.quizQuestion: In a perfectly competitive industry earning short-run economic profit, what long-run adjustment process restores this profit?
facts.explanation: Economic profits attract new firms, which increases industry supply and drives the market price down. This process continues until price equals ATC and economic profit returns to zero, the long-run equilibrium.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Firms exit, supply decreases, and price rises until losses are eliminated
acceptableAlternatives: exit drives price up to ATC
distractors: Firms enter, supply rises, price falls to AVC
Government sets a price floor to restore profit
Demand increases until price equals ATC

facts.quizQuestion: In a perfectly competitive industry suffering short-run negative profits, what long-run adjustment process restores normal profits?
facts.explanation: When firms earn economic losses, some exit the industry. Fewer firms means lower market supply, which drives price upward. Exit continues until price rises back to ATC and economic losses disappear.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: P = MR = MC = ATC (zero economic profit)
acceptableAlternatives: P = MC = ATC with zero profit
facts.quizQuestion: What four-way equality defines long-run equilibrium for a perfectly competitive firm, and what does it imply about economic profit?
facts.explanation: In long-run competitive equilibrium, P = MR = MC = ATC. Price equals marginal cost (allocative efficiency) and equals minimum ATC (productive efficiency). Economic profit is exactly zero because total revenue equals total cost.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: producing at minimum average total cost
facts.quizQuestion: What type of efficiency is achieved when a firm produces at the minimum point of its average total cost curve?
facts.explanation: Productive efficiency means a firm is producing at the lowest possible cost per unit, which occurs at the minimum of the ATC curve. In long-run competitive equilibrium, P = minimum ATC, so productive efficiency is automatically achieved.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of efficiency is achieved when price equals marginal cost, indicating that resources are directed to their highest-valued uses?
facts.explanation: Allocative efficiency occurs when P = MC, meaning the last unit produced is valued by consumers exactly as much as it costs society to produce. Perfect competition achieves allocative efficiency in long-run equilibrium because P = MC holds.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: Which market structure achieves both productive efficiency (P = min ATC) and allocative efficiency (P = MC) in long-run equilibrium?
facts.explanation: Perfect competition is the only market structure that simultaneously achieves productive and allocative efficiency in the long run. The entry and exit mechanism drives price to minimum ATC while also ensuring P = MC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What level of economic profit does a firm earn in long-run competitive equilibrium?
facts.explanation: In the long run, free entry and exit drive economic profit to zero. Firms still earn normal profit, which covers all opportunity costs, but there are no above-normal returns. This is why the long-run equilibrium condition is P = ATC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of market structure has a perfectly horizontal long-run supply curve because input prices do not change as the sector expands?
facts.explanation: In a constant-cost industry, expanding the number of firms does not bid up input prices, so each firm's cost curves remain unchanged. The long-run equilibrium price stays constant as industry output grows, producing a flat long-run supply curve.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of this has an upward-sloping long-run supply curve because entry bids up the price of specialized inputs?
facts.explanation: In an increasing-cost industry, as more firms enter (attracted by profits), demand for inputs rises, pushing input prices higher. This raises every firm's cost curves, resulting in a higher long-run equilibrium price and an upward-sloping long-run supply curve.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What term describes a that has already been paid and cannot be recovered, regardless of future production decisions?
facts.explanation: A sunk cost is an irreversible expenditure that should not influence future decisions because it cannot be recouped. In the short run, fixed costs are sunk — the firm has already committed to them regardless of whether it produces.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What cost concept represents the additional cost of producing one more unit of output?
facts.explanation: Marginal cost is the change in total cost resulting from producing one additional unit. It is the key cost used in the profit-maximization rule (MR = MC) and in deriving the firm's supply curve.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What cost concept equals total cost divided by quantity, and is used to determine whether a firm is earning profit or a loss?
facts.explanation: Average total cost is TC divided by Q, giving the cost per unit. Comparing ATC to price reveals profitability: if P > ATC the firm profits, if P < ATC the firm loses money, and if P = ATC the firm breaks even.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What cost concept equals total variable cost divided by quantity, and is the critical threshold for the shutdown decision?
facts.explanation: Average variable cost is TVC divided by Q. It represents the variable cost per unit of output and is the benchmark for the shutdown rule: if price falls below minimum AVC, the firm shuts down because it cannot cover even its variable production costs.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of profit subtracts both explicit and implicit (opportunity) costs from total revenue, and drives entry into a competitive industry?
facts.explanation: Economic profit equals total revenue minus all costs, including both explicit (monetary) and implicit (opportunity) costs. Positive economic profit signals that resources earn above their next-best use, attracting new firms to enter the industry.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: zero economic profit but all costs covered
facts.quizQuestion: What term describes the minimum return needed to keep a firm in an industry, equivalent to zero economic profit?
facts.explanation: Normal profit is earned when economic profit is zero, meaning all explicit and implicit costs are exactly covered. It is the required return to keep the owner in the business rather than pursuing their next-best alternative.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What characteristic of goods in perfect competition means buyers have no reason to prefer one seller's product over another's?
facts.explanation: In perfect competition, all firms sell identical (homogeneous) products. Since goods are indistinguishable, consumers base their choice entirely on price, which forces all firms to accept the same market price.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: no barriers to entry or exit
facts.quizQuestion: What this and exit condition enables long-run competitive equilibrium to be restored after short-run profit or loss?
facts.explanation: Free entry and exit means no legal, financial, or technological barriers prevent firms from entering a profitable industry or leaving an unprofitable one. This condition is what drives economic profit to zero in the long run.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

acceptableAlternatives: all buyers and sellers know prices
facts.quizQuestion: What informational assumption of perfect competition means buyers and sellers are fully aware of prices, costs, and available goods?
facts.explanation: Perfect information means all market participants know current prices, production costs, and product characteristics. This assumption prevents price dispersion and ensures that firms cannot charge above the market price without losing all customers.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Consumer surplus plus producer surplus is maximized
acceptableAlternatives: sum of CS and PS is greatest
facts.quizQuestion: What happens to total economic welfare (the sum of consumer benefit and producer benefit) in a perfectly competitive long-run equilibrium?
facts.explanation: In competitive equilibrium, P = MC ensures allocative efficiency, which means total surplus is maximized and there is no deadweight loss. Resources flow to their highest-valued uses, so no mutually beneficial trade is left unexploited.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cost remains constant regardless of how much output a firm produces in the short run?
facts.explanation: Fixed costs do not vary with output in the short run. Examples include rent, insurance premiums, and loan payments. Because they cannot be avoided in the short run, fixed costs are considered sunk and are irrelevant to the shutdown decision.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What type of cost changes with the level of output a firm produces?
facts.explanation: Variable costs change as output changes. Examples include raw materials, hourly labor, and energy. In the short run, variable costs are avoidable — if the firm shuts down, it pays no variable costs — which is why they are central to the shutdown decision.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What graph point corresponds to productive efficiency, where a firm achieves the lowest possible cost per unit of output?
facts.explanation: Productive efficiency is achieved at the minimum point of the ATC curve, where cost per unit is lowest. In perfect competition's long-run equilibrium, market forces push every firm to produce at this efficient scale.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: The MC curve intersects ATC at its minimum point
acceptableAlternatives: MC crosses ATC at minimum ATC
distractors: MC curve intersects AVC at its maximum point
ATC intersects AVC at its minimum point

facts.quizQuestion: What geometric relationship always holds between the marginal cost curve and the average total cost curve?
facts.explanation: The MC curve always passes through the minimum point of the ATC curve. When MC is below ATC, it pulls the average down; when MC is above ATC, it pulls the average up. The two are equal only at the ATC minimum.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: The MC curve intersects AVC at its minimum point
acceptableAlternatives: MC crosses AVC at minimum AVC

facts.quizQuestion: What geometric relationship holds between the marginal cost curve and the average variable cost curve?
facts.explanation: The MC curve passes through the minimum point of the AVC curve for the same mathematical reason it intersects minimum ATC: when MC is below AVC, the average falls; when MC exceeds AVC, the average rises. This minimum point is the shutdown point.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Many small firms with no individual market power
acceptableAlternatives: many firms each too small to affect price
distractors: A small number of large firms dominating the market
One firm controlling the entire market

facts.quizQuestion: What describes the seller-side structure of a perfectly competitive market in terms of the number and size of this?
facts.explanation: Perfect competition requires many small firms, each so small relative to the total market that no individual firm's output decision can affect the market price. This is the structural basis for price-taking behavior.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What happens to deadweight loss in a perfectly competitive long-run equilibrium where P = MC?
facts.explanation: When price equals marginal cost, all mutually beneficial trades occur and none are blocked. No deadweight loss exists because the market produces the socially optimal quantity where the marginal benefit to consumers equals the marginal cost to producers.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.correctAnswer: Increase quantity if MR exceeds MC; reduce quantity if MC exceeds MR
acceptableAlternatives: expand when MR > MC, contract when MC > MR
distractors: Increase quantity if ATC exceeds AVC
Reduce quantity if ATC exceeds AVC
Increase quantity if TR exceeds TC
Reduce quantity if P exceeds MR
Produce more if TR is rising
Stop producing when P equals ATC
Increase output if AFC is falling
Reduce output if total profit is positive
facts.quizQuestion: How does the profit-maximization rule guide a firm's output decision when comparing marginal revenue and marginal cost?
facts.explanation: When MR exceeds MC, producing one more unit adds more to revenue than to cost, raising profit, so the firm should expand. When MC exceeds MR, the last unit costs more than it earns, lowering profit, so the firm should reduce output. Profit is maximized where MR = MC.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Microeconomics CED, Unit 3

facts.quizQuestion: What market structure has a single seller offering a unique product with no close substitutes and high barriers to entry?
facts.explanation: A monopoly is the extreme of market power: one firm is the entire industry. Because there are no substitutes, the monopolist faces the downward-sloping market demand curve directly and acts as a price maker.

facts.quizQuestion: What type of market structure arises when one firm can supply the entire market at a lower cost than two or more firms, because LRATC falls throughout the range of market demand?
facts.explanation: A natural monopoly occurs when economies of scale are so large relative to market demand that a single firm has the lowest average costs. Splitting the market between two firms would raise costs for each — making one firm the efficient outcome.

facts.quizQuestion: What are the obstacles that prevent new firms from entering a market and competing with an established monopolist called?
facts.explanation: Barriers to entry protect a monopolist's market power by blocking potential competitors. They come in three main forms: legal (patents, licenses), natural (economies of scale), and strategic (brand loyalty, exclusive contracts).

facts.quizQuestion: Which type of legal barrier to entry grants an inventor the exclusive right to produce and sell an innovation for a fixed period of time?
facts.explanation: Patents are government-issued legal barriers that give inventors a temporary monopoly — typically 20 years — in exchange for publicly disclosing the innovation. Drug companies, for example, hold patents that prevent generic competitors from entering the market.

facts.quizQuestion: What type of barrier to entry creates a natural monopoly by causing long-run average total costs to fall continuously as output increases across the entire range of market demand?
facts.explanation: When LRATC falls throughout the range of demand, one large firm can always undercut any smaller entrant on cost. This natural cost advantage forms the economic basis for natural monopolies like electric utilities and water systems.

facts.quizQuestion: Unlike a perfectly competitive firm, a monopolist is called a what — because it can set its own price rather than accepting the market price?
facts.explanation: A monopolist faces the entire downward-sloping market demand curve, so it chooses both quantity and price simultaneously. It is a price maker because no rival firm exists to undercut it — though the demand curve still constrains the price it can charge.

facts.correctAnswer: MR < P (marginal revenue is less than price)
distractors: MR = MC (marginal revenue equals marginal cost)
MR = ATC (marginal revenue equals average total cost)
P = AVC (price equals average variable cost)
facts.quizQuestion: For a monopolist facing a downward-sloping demand curve, how does marginal revenue compare to price for any positive level of output?
facts.explanation: To sell one more unit, a monopolist must lower its price on ALL units sold, not just the marginal one. The revenue gained from the new unit is partially offset by the revenue lost on previous units, so MR is always below the demand curve (price).

facts.correctAnswer: twice the slope of the demand curve
acceptableAlternatives: slope twice as steep as demand
distractors: the same slope as the demand curve
half the slope of the demand curve
a slope equal to marginal cost
